topic: Articles 20-22: Criminal Safeguards, Life and Preventive Detention
subtopic: Ex post facto law, double jeopardy, self-incrimination, due process, arrest safeguards and detention

content.issueStory: Liberty is tested most severely when the State investigates, punishes or detains; constitutional safeguards therefore distinguish conviction, procedure, arrest and preventive detention. Article 20 protects against retrospective criminal penalty, double punishment for the same offence and compelled testimonial self-incrimination. Article 21 requires life or personal liberty to be deprived only by just, fair and reasonable procedure. Article 22 regulates arrest and creates a distinct preventive-detention framework.
content.coreConcept: Article 20 protects against retrospective criminal penalty, double punishment for the same offence and compelled testimonial self-incrimination. Article 21 requires life or personal liberty to be deprived only by just, fair and reasonable procedure. Article 22 regulates arrest and creates a distinct preventive-detention framework.
dimensions: Article 20 protections apply in criminal contexts and include both citizens and non-citizens.
Ex post facto protection bars harsher retrospective criminal liability, not every retrospective civil consequence.
Double jeopardy under Article 20(2) requires prosecution and punishment for the same offence.
Self-incrimination protects an accused from compelled testimonial evidence, not every physical sample.
Article 21 has expanded through due-process, dignity, privacy, livelihood and fair-procedure doctrine.
Article 22 gives arrest safeguards while clauses on preventive detention permit exceptional detention with procedural controls.
content.constitutionalLink: Articles 20-22; Maneka Gandhi, K. S. Puttaswamy, D. K. Basu and dated preventive-detention jurisprudence.
content.dataReport: Uploaded source basis: STATIC_09_Polity.pdf and PYQ_06_Polity_Governance.pdf. Refresh amendments, statutory rules, office status and later judgments from official sources: Constitution of India, India Code and Supreme Court judgment database.
content.caseStudy: Maneka Gandhi linked Articles 14, 19 and 21 and required procedure affecting liberty to be fair, just and reasonable rather than merely enacted.
argumentsFor: Article 21 protects dignity through fair procedure.
argumentsAgainst: Preventive detention permits confinement without ordinary criminal trial.
Security exceptions can weaken practical access to counsel and review.
Doctrine depends on effective remedies and institutions.
content.pyqLink: UPSC patterns test Article-institution matches, exceptions, procedure and comparative statements within Articles 20-22: Criminal Safeguards, Life and Preventive Detention. Resolve each statement through source, scope, procedure and consequence.

answerFramework.intro: Introduce Articles 20-22: Criminal Safeguards, Life and Preventive Detention through the controlling constitutional principle and legal source.
body: Map the authority: Articles 20-22; Maneka Gandhi, K. S. Puttaswamy, D. K. Basu and dated preventive-detention jurisprudence.

Explain the first-order design: Article 20 protections apply in criminal contexts and include both citizens and non-citizens. Ex post facto protection bars harsher retrospective criminal liability, not every retrospective civil consequence. Double jeopardy under Article 20(2) requires prosecution and punishment for the same offence.

Analyse safeguards and tensions: Self-incrimination protects an accused from compelled testimonial evidence, not every physical sample. Article 21 has expanded through due-process, dignity, privacy, livelihood and fair-procedure doctrine. Article 22 gives arrest safeguards while clauses on preventive detention permit exceptional detention with procedural controls.

Use Maneka Gandhi linked Articles 14, 19 and 21 and required procedure affecting liberty to be fair, just and reasonable rather than merely enacted. and close with constitutional accountability plus dated legal verification.
answerFramework.conclusion: A strong Polity answer joins constitutional text, institutional purpose, judicial doctrine and a workable accountability safeguard.

mainsExamples: Arrested person produced before magistrate under ordinary arrest rules.
Preventive detention reviewed under its special constitutional-statute framework.
prelimsFacts: Article 20(2) concerns prosecution and punishment for the same offence.
Article 20(3) concerns an accused and compelled testimonial evidence.
Article 21 protects life and personal liberty.
Preventive detention is treated separately within Article 22.

revisionBox.answer: Article 20 protects against retrospective criminal penalty, double punishment for the same offence and compelled testimonial self-incrimination. Article 21 requires life or personal liberty to be deprived only by just, fair and reasonable procedure. Article 22 regulates arrest and creates a distinct preventive-detention framework.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the primary constitutional or legal authority?
revisionBox.answer: Articles 20-22; Maneka Gandhi, K. S. Puttaswamy, D. K. Basu and dated preventive-detention jurisprudence.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the fastest elimination trap?
revisionBox.answer: Do not apply ex post facto protection to every civil law.

revisionBox.prompt: Where should later legal changes be verified?
revisionBox.answer: Constitution of India, India Code and Supreme Court judgment database

story: Liberty is tested most severely when the State investigates, punishes or detains; constitutional safeguards therefore distinguish conviction, procedure, arrest and preventive detention.
core_concept: Article 20 protects against retrospective criminal penalty, double punishment for the same offence and compelled testimonial self-incrimination. Article 21 requires life or personal liberty to be deprived only by just, fair and reasonable procedure. Article 22 regulates arrest and creates a distinct preventive-detention framework.

mindmap_json.center: Articles 20-22: Criminal Safeguards, Life and Preventive Detention

branches.label: Text
children: Article 20 protections apply in criminal contexts and include both citizens and non-citizens.
Ex post facto protection bars harsher retrospective criminal liability, not every retrospective civil consequence.

branches.label: Institution
children: Double jeopardy under Article 20(2) requires prosecution and punishment for the same offence.
Self-incrimination protects an accused from compelled testimonial evidence, not every physical sample.

branches.label: Safeguard
children: Article 21 has expanded through due-process, dignity, privacy, livelihood and fair-procedure doctrine.
Article 22 gives arrest safeguards while clauses on preventive detention permit exceptional detention with procedural controls.

branches.label: Traps
children: Do not apply ex post facto protection to every civil law.
Do not equate all physical evidence with testimonial compulsion.
Do not apply ordinary arrest safeguards identically to every preventive-detention clause.

key_facts: Article 20(2) concerns prosecution and punishment for the same offence.
Article 20(3) concerns an accused and compelled testimonial evidence.
Article 21 protects life and personal liberty.
Preventive detention is treated separately within Article 22.
common_traps: Do not apply ex post facto protection to every civil law.
Do not equate all physical evidence with testimonial compulsion.
Do not apply ordinary arrest safeguards identically to every preventive-detention clause.
